channel.title: UFO on Alien Truth Social
channel.description: Recent content in UFO on Alien Truth Social

item.title: British UFO Research Association
item.description:
The British UFO Research Association (BUFORA) is a UK organization dedicated to investigating UFO phenomena in the British Isles. Founded in 1964, it reportedly had around 1,000 members in 1997 and investigates over 400 cases annually, with 95% identified as hoaxes. BUFORA has held annual conferences since 1987 and supports witnesses who believe they have encountered extraterrestrials. The organization has also been critical of the UK government’s handling of UFO-related information under the Freedom of Information Act.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_UFO_Research_Association

item.title: National UFO Reporting Center
item.description:
The National UFO Reporting Center (NUFORC) is a nonprofit corporation in Washington State that documents UFO and UAP sightings. Founded in 1974, it has cataloged nearly 170,000 reported sightings, primarily in the United States. The center provides statistics, graphs, and maps to aid in research and offers a 24-hour hotline and online form for submitting reports. Peter Davenport has served as director since 1994, and the organization is largely funded by Davenport and Christian Stepien.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_UFO_Reporting_Center

item.title: Rendlesham Forest incident
item.description:
The Rendlesham Forest incident involved reported sightings of unexplained lights near Rendlesham Forest in Suffolk, England, in December 1980. USAF personnel claimed to have seen a UFO, and the event is often called “Britain’s Roswell.” Sceptics suggest the sightings were misinterpretations of a fireball, the Orfordness Lighthouse, and bright stars. The UK Ministry of Defence stated the event posed no threat to national security and was not investigated as such.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rendlesham_Forest_incident

item.title: National Investigations Committee On Aerial Phenomena
item.description:
The National Investigations Committee On Aerial Phenomena (NICAP) was a UFO research organization active in the United States from 1956 to 1980. Despite facing financial difficulties, NICAP gained significant visibility and respectability, advocating for scientific investigation of UFO sightings and arguing for a governmental cover-up of UFO evidence. The organization’s membership fluctuated, peaking in the early 1960s before declining sharply after the publication of the Condon Report. NICAP officially dissolved in 1980, and its archives were later acquired by the Center for UFO Studies.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Investigations_Committee_On_Aerial_Phenomena

item.title: The Black Vault Launches Searchable UFO Files Release Archive
item.description:
The Black Vault has launched a new searchable archive for the recently released UFO Files Release #1 collection. This archive, built on a custom search engine, makes the government’s UAP document release more accessible than the original format. The Black Vault processed the files to remove password restrictions and improve searchability, including generating searchable text from documents. The platform offers direct access to records, with features like searchable PDFs, videos, and metadata, and will expand as new UAP records are released.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/the-black-vault-launches-searchable-ufo-files-release-archive

item.title: Close encounter
item.description:
In ufology, a “close encounter” is defined as witnessing an unidentified flying object (UFO) at a close range, reducing the likelihood of misidentification. This classification system was first proposed by J. Allen Hynek in his 1972 book, The UFO Experience: A Scientific Inquiry. Hynek’s original scale included six levels, ranging from nocturnal lights to encounters involving animated entities. Later, Jacques Vallée expanded this scale to include close encounters of the fourth and fifth kinds, with the latter referring to human-initiated contact with extraterrestrial life.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Close_encounter

item.title: Glenn Dennis
item.description:
Glenn Dennis, born March 24, 1925, and died April 28, 2015, was a founder of the International UFO Museum and Research Center in Roswell, New Mexico. He began working at Ballard Funeral Home in Roswell at age 15 and later became responsible for the funeral home’s military contract with Roswell Army Air Field. Dennis gained attention from UFO researchers in 1994 after contacting a tips hotline following an episode of Unsolved Mysteries about the Roswell UFO incident. While his accounts were featured in books about the incident, serious doubts were raised about his testimony, with creators of a documentary discovering he provided a fictitious name for a nurse who allegedly gave him classified information.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glenn_Dennis

item.title: Unidentified flying object
item.description:
An unidentified flying object (UFO), also referred to as an unidentified anomalous phenomenon (UAP), is any aerial object or phenomenon that cannot be immediately identified or explained. While historical reports of unusual aerial sightings date back to ancient times, the term gained cultural prominence following World War II, leading to various government investigations, such as the U.S. Air Force’s Project Blue Book. Although scientific and skeptical organizations, including the Committee for Skeptical Inquiry, consistently find that the majority of these reports are misidentifications of natural phenomena, human technology, or hoaxes, a small percentage remain unexplained. In recent years, entities such as NASA’s UAP independent study team and the Department of Defense’s All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office have continued to analyze data regarding these encounters.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unidentified_flying_object

item.title: Citizens Against UFO Secrecy
item.description:
Founded in 1977, Citizens Against UFO Secrecy (CAUS) was a non-profit organization that utilized the Freedom of Information Act to advocate for the public release of classified government documents concerning UFOs. Under the leadership of attorney Peter Gersten, the group successfully secured over 900 documents from the CIA in 1979 but faced legal setbacks in subsequent attempts to obtain files from the National Security Agency and state officials. As of 2023, the organization is listed as inactive with its non-profit status revoked.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Citizens_Against_UFO_Secrecy

item.title: Mutilation of "Snippy" the horse
item.description:
The 1967 death of an Appaloosa mare named “Snippy” near Alamosa, Colorado, gained national attention after reports suggested the animal had been mysteriously mutilated by extraterrestrial entities. While sensationalized media accounts linked the incident to UFOs, later investigations by the Condon Committee and others attributed the death to natural causes and gunshot wounds inflicted by local individuals. Despite these findings, the case remains a foundational element of UFO folklore and the study of alleged animal mutilations. The horse’s skeletal remains were ultimately acquired by the UFO Watchtower in 2021 as a tourist attraction.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mutilation_of_%22Snippy%22_the_horse

item.title: William L. Moore and His Contributions to UFOlogy
item.description:
William L. Moore, born in 1943, is an author and former UFO researcher known for his work from the late 1970s to the late 1980s. He co-authored books with Charles Berlitz, including “The Philadelphia Experiment - Project Invisibility” and “The Roswell Incident,” which alleged extraterrestrial involvement in the Roswell incident. Moore also circulated the Majestic 12 documents, which purported to reveal a secret government group overseeing UFOs. Critics have described his books as unsubstantiated and his work on the Majestic 12 documents as fraudulent.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bill_Moore_%28ufologist%29

item.title: International UFO Museum and Research Center
item.description:
The International UFO Museum and Research Center is located in Roswell, New Mexico, and was established in 1991 as a nonprofit educational organization. It is largely focused on the 1947 Roswell incident and other UFO events. The museum features exhibits on UFO encounters and an extensive library of related materials. It also serves as a central point for the annual UFOfest held in Roswell.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_UFO_Museum_and_Research_Center

item.title: GEIPAN: Study and Information Group on Unidentified Aerospace Phenomena
item.description:
GEIPAN, the Unidentified Aerospace Phenomena Research and Information Group, is a unit of the French space agency CNES. Founded in 1977 as GEPAN, it has undergone several name changes and is dedicated to investigating unidentified aerospace phenomena and making its findings public. The organization’s mission includes drawing on data from reports, including those channeled by the French Gendarmerie, and utilizing CNES’s technical resources for scientific investigation. GEIPAN began making its archives available online in March 2007, and recent reports estimate that around 3% of sightings remain unidentified.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GEIPAN

item.title: Hoaxes Related to Unidentified Flying Objects
item.description:
Numerous hoaxes related to the study of unidentified flying objects have been perpetrated throughout history. These include practical jokes like a balloon with a burning basket in 1897 and a turkey vulture with a burning cotton ball, which led to a school fire. Other hoaxes involved fabricated accounts of airships, such as one where a cow was reportedly lifted by a ship, and the controversial “alien autopsy” film footage that was later revealed to be a hoax. Photographic hoaxes, like those of George Adamski and a purported alien at Ilkley Moor, have also been exposed.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_UFO-related_hoaxes

item.title: Solway Firth Spaceman: The Enigmatic Figure in Jim Templeton's Photograph
item.description:
The Solway Firth Spaceman refers to a figure in a photograph taken on May 23, 1964, by Jim Templeton. Templeton claimed the figure, appearing in a spacesuit, was not visible when he took the picture. Journalist David Clarke suggested the figure was Templeton’s wife, whose blue dress was overexposed to appear white. Despite Templeton’s insistence on the photo’s authenticity, analysts confirmed it was genuine, and the figure has been widely debated by ufologists and paranormal investigators.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solway_Firth_Spaceman

item.title: The Seekers: A UFO Religion in Mid-Twentieth Century United States
item.description:
The Seekers, also known as The Brotherhood of the Seven Rays, was a UFO religion founded in 1953 by Charles Laughead in the Midwestern United States. Led by Dorothy Martin, the group believed flying saucers would save them from a predicted catastrophe on December 21, 1954. When the prophecy failed, many members experienced cognitive dissonance, a phenomenon studied by Leon Festinger in his book When Prophecy Fails. The Seekers are considered by some to be the earliest UFO religion.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Seekers_%28rapturists%29

item.title: Puerto Rico Highway 303
item.description:
Puerto Rico Highway 303 is a rural road connecting Cabo Rojo and Lajas. A section of this highway, spanning the southern border of Sierra Bermeja, is known as the “Puerto Rico Alien Route” due to numerous UFO reports in the area. While a sign designating it as the “Ruta Extraterrestre” was installed, it was removed after the Puerto Rico Department of Transportation and Public Works rejected official recognition. The municipality of Lajas and local businesses now promote this route as a tourist attraction.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Puerto_Rico_Highway_303

item.title: Area 51: The Dreamland Chronicles
item.description:
Area 51: The Dreamland Chronicles is a 1997 non-fiction book by David Darlington about Area 51 in Nevada. Reviews noted the book’s service in detailing the site’s early history while also criticizing its inclusion of accounts from “alien seekers” and “saucer nuts.” The book has been called a definitive work on Area 51 and its cultural impact, with one review describing it as even-handed towards ufologists. It was published by Henry Holt & Company.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Area_51:_The_Dreamland_Chronicles

item.title: UFO Watchtower
item.description:
The UFO Watchtower, located north of Hooper, Colorado, is an observation platform and campground that opened in May 2000. It was established by cattle farmer Judy Messoline, who converted her barn into a tourist attraction to capitalize on reported UFO sightings in the San Luis Valley. The attraction relies on donations and sales of alien-themed merchandise for funding. Over 30,000 visitors have reportedly visited the Watchtower since its opening, with reported sightings and a “healing garden” also being features.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_Watchtower

item.title: FOIA Request on UAP Keywords Produces Minimal Results, Heavy Redactions, from Energy Department
item.description:
A Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request to the U.S. Department of Energy for communications regarding unidentified aerial phenomena (UAP) yielded only two partially released documents. These documents, internal DOE emails from May 2024, were prepared for Secretary Jennifer Granholm in advance of an oversight hearing. The emails reference attached briefing materials that included “UFO and UPA talking points” for congressional engagement. Despite the request’s broad scope, significant portions of the released documents were withheld under FOIA exemptions, preventing the disclosure of substantive content or internal analysis on UAP topics.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/foia-request-on-uap-keywords-produces-minimal-results-heavy-redactions-from-energy-department

item.title: AATIP Resurfaces in 2022 TS-SCI Briefing, According to Navy FOIA Release
item.description:
Two Freedom of Information Act requests to the Department of the Navy yielded a single responsive record: an email chain detailing a March 2022 briefing on the Advance Aerospace Threat Identification Program (AATIP) and the Airborne Object Identification and Management Synchronization Group (AOIMSG). This briefing, held at Roosevelt Hall on Fort McNair, included classified TS-SCI sessions and discussed topics such as congressional reports and “Tic-Tac” incidents. Despite redactions, the record’s responsiveness to requests specifically seeking information involving Brennan P. McKernan and Luis Elizondo suggests their names were present in the communications. The reappearance of AATIP in a 2022 briefing, alongside its successor AOIMSG, complicates previous characterizations of the program’s scope and Elizondo’s role.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/aatip-resurfaces-in-2022-ts-sci-briefing-according-to-navy-foia-release

item.title: Center for UFO Studies (CUFOS)
item.description:
The Center for UFO Studies (CUFOS) is a privately funded UFO research group founded in 1973 by J. Allen Hynek. Hynek, who had previously consulted for the U.S. Air Force’s Project Blue Book, established CUFOS to continue the scientific study of UFO reports after the project’s closure. The organization maintains a library and archives of UFO-related materials and compiles and analyzes UFO incident reports from around the world. CUFOS is now based in Chicago, Illinois, and its case files were moved to Albuquerque, New Mexico, in 2020.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Center_for_UFO_Studies

item.title: Despite Trump’s Call to Release UAP Files, Navy Denies Appeal for 78 Classified UAP Photographs
item.description:
The U.S. Navy has formally denied an appeal seeking the release of 78 photographs designated as “unidentified aerial phenomena” (UAP). This decision upholds a previous denial of a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request filed by The Black Vault. The Navy cited classification under Executive Order 13526 and the UAP Classification Guide as the reason for withholding the images. Despite public calls for transparency and a former president’s stated desire to release such files, the Navy’s appeal authority deferred to the Original Classification Authority, stating that no portion of the photographs could be released.

Source: https://www.theblackvault.com/documentarchive/despite-trumps-call-to-release-uap-files-navy-denies-appeal-for-78-classified-uap-photographs

item.title: 1967 Malmstrom Air Force Base UFO Incident
item.description:
In 1996, retired Air Force personnel claimed a 1967 weapons failure at a Montana nuclear missile complex was linked to a UFO sighting over Malmstrom Air Force Base, an event known as the Malmstrom UFO incident. Skeptics suggest the UFO was likely the planet Mars and unrelated to the malfunction, and a military investigation found no connection to any UFO sighting. However, a 2025 Pentagon report revealed the incident was caused by a classified test of an electromagnetic pulse device.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malmstrom_UFO_incident

item.title: UFO Coverup? Live
item.description:
UFO Coverup? Live was a US television broadcast that aired on October 14, 1988, and introduced the Majestic 12 hoax and Area 51 to Americans. Hosted by Mike Farrell, the program discussed various UFO incidents and featured interviews with ufologists, pilots, and skeptics. It also included clips from films with alien themes and presented claims from informants about aliens and a government agreement for an Area 51 base. The program later faced criticism for its truthfulness and was noted for influencing The X-Files.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_Cover_Up%3F_Live

item.title: Ängelholm UFO memorial
item.description:
The Ängelholm UFO memorial in Sweden commemorates an alleged UFO landing site. It features a concrete model of a flying saucer and landing impressions, based on the account of Gösta Carlsson. Carlsson claimed a UFO encounter in 1946 led to his business success, though investigations have not corroborated his story. Despite the lack of evidence, the monument, erected in 1972, has become a tourist attraction and is maintained by the local government.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%84ngelholm_UFO_memorial

item.title: International UFO Congress
item.description:
The International UFO Congress is an annual conference established in 1991 in Arizona that focuses on ufology. It moved to the Phoenix, Arizona area in 2011 after previously being held in Laughlin, Nevada. The event features presentations on topics like alien abduction, UFO sightings, and paranormal experiences, with speakers from around the world. The conference has changed ownership multiple times since its inception.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_UFO_Congress

item.title: World UFO Day
item.description:
World UFO Day is an awareness day observed annually by some on June 24 and others on July 2. The June date commemorates the first widely reported UFO sighting in the United States, while the July date marks the Roswell incident. The July 2 observance, declared by the World UFO Day Organisation, aims to raise awareness of UFOs and encourage governments to declassify related files. Celebrations involve sky-watching and discussions about the possibility of extraterrestrial life.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_UFO_Day

item.title: Marfa lights
item.description:
The Marfa lights are an optical phenomenon observed near Marfa, Texas, often seen from a publicized viewing area. Scientific investigations between 2000 and 2008 concluded that these lights are primarily caused by automobile headlights distorted by warm desert air. Historical accounts of the lights date back to 1883, and they have been featured in various media and inspired artistic works.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marfa_lights

item.title: SOBEPS: Belgian Society for the Study of Space Phenomena
item.description:
SOBEPS, the Belgian society for the study of space phenomena, was formed in 1971 and gained fame for investigating the 1989-1990 Belgian UFO wave. The group published a magazine called Inforespace and saw its membership fluctuate significantly over the years. Following the wave of sightings, SOBEPS collaborated with the Belgian Air Force. The society dissolved in 2007 due to financial difficulties, with COBEPS formed to continue some of its activities.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SOBEPS

item.title: UFO Investigators League
item.description:
The UFO Investigators League (UFOIL) was a UFO group founded by Timothy Green Beckley in the early 1970s, which later re-emerged in 1990 in New Brunswick, New Jersey. Another chapter, founded in 1981 in Covington, Kentucky, investigated UFOs, ghosts, and Bigfoot sightings before disbanding in 1982. The Ohio UFO Investigators League (OUFOIL) operated a UFO hotline in 1973 from Fairfield, Ohio, and investigated various cases including the Loveland Lizard and Mothman. OUFOIL also hosted the 1978 MUFON Symposium and published a newsletter called “The Ohio Skywatcher.”

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFO_Investigators_League

item.title: Istanbul UFO Museum
item.description:
The Istanbul UFO Museum, established in 2001 in Beyoğlu, Istanbul, Turkey, was the fourth international UFO museum. Founded by Haktan Akdoğan’s Sirius UFO Space Sciences Research Center, it housed photos, videos, reports, UFO-alien models, and a library on UFOs and extraterrestrial life. The museum reportedly had a mobile version that traveled around Istanbul in a truck in 2011. The Istanbul UFO Museum is no longer open.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Istanbul_UFO_Museum

item.title: Yankee Blue: Part of Pentagon UFO Disinformation Campaign
item.description:
Yankee Blue was a fake United States Air Force program that purported to involve the reverse-engineering of alien spacecraft. In reality, it was an internal hazing and misinformation campaign, possibly part of a broader Pentagon disinformation program. For decades, personnel in classified Air Force units were shown “UFO” photos and told they were assisting in a top-secret alien-technology recovery program. This ritual persisted from the Cold War era until its discontinuation in 2023. The strategy likely originated as a security measure to protect sensitive programs and divert attention from advanced weapons development.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yankee_Blue

item.title: Cultural Tracking in UFO Reports
item.description:
In ufology, cultural tracking describes how UFO reports evolve to mirror contemporary cultural changes and technological advancements. Historically, reported UFOs have reflected the era’s technology, from mysterious airships in the 1890s to flying saucers resembling current aerospace designs. This phenomenon also influences UFO narratives by incorporating elements from prevalent science fiction, such as car engine stoppages or specific alien appearances. Cultural tracking challenges the extraterrestrial hypothesis, suggesting instead a psychosocial explanation where UFOs and their occupants are imaginary, though some proponents of the interdimensional hypothesis believe advanced beings deliberately alter their appearance.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cultural_tracking

item.title: Archives for the Unexplained
item.description:
Archives for the Unexplained (AFU), formerly Archives for UFO research, is a nonprofit foundation in Norrköping, Sweden, dedicated to collecting materials on UFOs, ufology, Forteana, cryptozoology, paranormal phenomena, and folklore. Founded in 1973 as the Work Group for Ufology, it was converted to a charitable foundation in 1980 and moved to its current location. AFU manages approximately 3.5 kilometers of shelf space containing vast collections of books, magazines, newspaper clippings, photographs, and reports, including Scandinavian and British archives. The organization also collects various related items like scale models, paintings, and memorabilia.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Archives_for_the_Unexplained

item.title: COMETA Report
item.description:
The COMETA report is an unofficial 1999 publication by the French association COMETA concerning unidentified aerospace phenomena (UAP). Titled “UFOs and Defense: What Should We Prepare For?”, the report compiled UAP/UFO incidents over approximately 60 years, focusing on national defense implications. Contributors included defense experts and former officials from the French Air Force and space agency. The report concluded that a significant percentage of well-documented cases could not be explained by known sources, suggesting the extraterrestrial hypothesis as the most probable explanation for certain phenomena.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/COMETA_report

item.title: Integratron
item.description:
The Integratron is a cupola structure in Landers, California, designed by ufologist George Van Tassel, who claimed it could rejuvenate, provide anti-gravity, and enable time travel. Van Tassel stated he received instructions for its construction from Venusians and began building it in 1957, with the structure erected in 1959. After Van Tassel’s death, the building was acquired by sisters who now offer “sound baths” within the structure, promoting it as an acoustically perfect sound chamber. The Integratron was added to the National Register of Historic Places in April 2018.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Integratron

item.title: Civilian Saucer Intelligence
item.description:
Civilian Saucer Intelligence (CSI) was an independent UFO research group founded in New York City in 1954. Unlike many amateur groups, CSI conducted rigorous investigations and published a well-regarded quarterly newsletter. The organization was critical of contactees but distinct in its investigation of close encounters involving alleged animate beings. CSI’s core personnel included Ted Bloecher, Isabel Davis, and Alexander Mebane, and the group was instrumental in translating French ufologist Aimé Michel’s books into English. CSI was defunct by 1959, though Davis and Bloecher remained active in UFO research.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Civilian_Saucer_Intelligence

item.title: The Martian Monkey Hoax
item.description:
In 1953 Atlanta, Edward Watters, Tom Wilson, and Arnold Payne perpetrated a hoax using a dead rhesus monkey. They altered the monkey’s appearance and created scorch marks to suggest it was from a flying saucer. The prank occurred during a period of UFO hysteria, leading to public belief and an Air Force investigation. The hoax was exposed by an anatomy professor, and the perpetrators eventually confessed, with Watters paying a fine for obstructing a highway. The “Martian Monkey” is now displayed at the Georgia Bureau of Investigation headquarters.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martian_Monkey

item.title: Unidentified Submerged Object
item.description:
An unidentified submerged object (USO) is an object that is unidentified and submerged in water. The U.S. Navy categorizes USOs as marine life that can disrupt naval operations. The term USO has also been used by ufologists to describe unidentified flying objects that may also travel underwater.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unidentified_submerged_object

item.title: Cardwell UFO Festival: A Mysterious Event in Australia
item.description:
The Cardwell UFO Festival, held annually in Cardwell, Queensland, is Australia’s sole UFO festival. Established in 2014, it serves as a platform to discuss local unusual sightings and activities. The festival features entertainment, family activities, markets, food, and an alien-themed costume party. It also includes “The C-Files,” a forum for attendees to share their UFO experiences and other mysterious sightings. In 2017, the festival was recognized as a Top 10 Festival for UFO and Alien Lovers and one of Australia’s 7 Most Eyebrow-Raising Festivals.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cardwell_UFO_Festival

item.title: Vision Ovni
item.description:
Vision Ovni is an Argentine national UFO research organization founded in 1991. It began operations in Victoria, Entre Rios, in mid-1991, coinciding with press reports of strange lights from Laguna del Pescado lake. The organization’s objectives include researching UFO phenomena, collecting and studying information on UFOs in Argentina, and applying scientific principles to investigations. In 2009, Vision Ovni was part of the formation of Cefora, an Argentine organization dedicated to studying the UFO phenomena in detail.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Visi%C3%B3n_Ovni

item.title: UFOetry in 2008, Joshua Poet on right
item.description:
UFOetry is a band from Los Angeles, California, formed in July 2004 by ufologist Joshua Poet. The band performs rock operas centered on UFO phenomena, combining live music and theatrical elements in their multimedia shows. They present their message at UFO festivals and conferences across the continental United States, aiming to spread awareness of UFO phenomena globally. UFOetry has received recognition from the Los Angeles music scene for various achievements, including music video and concert performance awards.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UFOetry

item.title: UFOCOM or Belarusian UFOlogical Community
item.description:
The UFOCOM, or Belarusian Ufological Community, is a non-commercial, non-government public organization founded in 2001 in Horki, Belarus. It originated from students studying paranormal phenomena and has grown into a statewide network of volunteers conducting research. Since 2005, UFOCOM has been part of the Kosmopoisk international movement, serving as a public node for UFO research in the CIS network. The organization focuses on studying anomalous phenomena, including UFOs, crop circles, and poltergeists, and conducts research in cooperation with the National Academy of Sciences of Belarus.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UfoCom

item.title: Wonsan-Sunchon UFO Incident
item.description:
On the night of January 29, 1952, during the Korean War, four American military personnel reported seeing an orange globe-shaped light over Wonsan and Sunchon. Air Force UFO experts believe this incident contributed to the 1952 UFO flap. The sightings occurred amid fears of new Soviet technology and during a stalemate in the war, with Wonsan under naval blockade and Sunchon experiencing a major air battle. The incident gained nationwide attention through United Press and was discussed in publications like Time and Life magazines, with the Air Force confirming an ongoing investigation.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wonsan-Sunchon_UFO_incident

item.title: Above Majestic
item.description:
Above Majestic is a 2018 documentary film that proposes a global conspiracy theory involving reptilian aliens who secretly control world governments. The film posits that these aliens inhabit Antarctica, are preparing for an invasion, and are behind the September 11 attacks as a means to hide their conspiracy. According to Vice, the documentary claims these aliens control governments and banks. Researchers reported in 2021 that a QAnon-linked Twitter user may have aided in the film’s production.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Above_Majestic

item.title: Fund for UFO Research (FUFOR)
item.description:
The Fund for UFO Research (FUFOR) was a non-profit UFO research group founded in 1979 in Mount Rainier, Maryland. Its stated goals were to advance scholarly research into UFOs and the extraterrestrial hypothesis, and to facilitate the release of classified U.S. government documents related to these topics. From 1979 to 2006, FUFOR provided over $700,000 in research grants and supported investigations into subjects like the MJ-12 papers and Project Blue Book. The organization has not had an active online or print presence since 2011.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fund_for_UFO_Research

item.title: Gilliland's Ranch
item.description:
Gilliland’s Ranch, also known as ECETI Ranch and Sattva Sanctuary, is located in Trout Lake, Washington, at the base of Mount Adams. The property owner, James Gilliland, claims to have established contact with extraterrestrial intelligence and has hosted UFO sighting events since 2003. Gilliland reports frequent UFO sightings and “unexplained light shows” at the location.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gilliland%27s_Ranch

item.title: Aerial Phenomena Research Organization
item.description:
The Aerial Phenomena Research Organization (APRO) was a UFO research group founded in January 1952. Based in Tucson, Arizona after 1960, APRO emphasized scientific investigations and employed consulting scientists, including James E. McDonald and James Harder. J. Allen Hynek considered APRO and NICAP to be the two most reputable civilian UFO groups of their time. A portion of APRO’s membership later formed the Mutual UFO Network (MUFON) in 1969. APRO’s case files were transferred to the National UFO Historical Records Center in November 2023.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aerial_Phenomena_Research_Organization

item.title: Green fireballs
item.description:
Green fireballs are a type of unidentified flying object reported since the early 1950s, primarily in the southwestern United States. Early reports from late 1948 described them as bright green balls of fire or like huge green meteors. While some ufologists suggest an extraterrestrial origin, mainstream explanations include natural bolides. Investigations in the late 1940s and early 1950s, including Project Twinkle, were unable to definitively identify the origin, with official conclusions leaning towards natural phenomena.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Green_fireballs

item.title: Civilian Saucer Investigation (CSI-LA)
item.description:
Civilian Saucer Investigation (CSI-LA) was a Los Angeles-based group that studied flying saucer and UFO reports in the early 1950s. The group was formed by three North American Aviation technical writers and a rocket scientist after they reported witnessing unusual aerial objects. CSI-LA was featured in Time and Life magazines and published a quarterly bulletin. The organization dissolved in October 1952 after one of its founders claimed the initial sighting was a joke.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Civilian_Saucer_Investigation

item.title: CEFORA: Argentinean UFO Phenomenon Studies Committee
item.description:
CEFORA, the Argentinean Republic Committee for UFO Phenomena Studies, is an organization formed by various ufology groups in Argentina. Its primary objective is the declassification of all UFO-related phenomena in Argentina. CEFORA has formally requested information from the National Ministry of Defense, the National Presidency, and the Chamber of Deputies. Silvia Pérez Simondini serves as the director of CEFORA and also runs the Visión OVNI UFO Museum in Victoria, Entre Ríos.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cefora
